---
title: "agent-sync manifest schema (v1)"
status: active
date: 2026-04-21
owner: internal/manifest
---

# agent-sync manifest schema — v1

The manifest is the single file that binds a workspace directory to its
canonical agent-config source. Exactly one manifest lives per workspace;
running `agent-sync sync` resolves the workspace by walking up from cwd (or
honoring `--workspace <path>`) and reading the file at `<root>/.agent-sync.yaml`.

This document is **authoritative** for the v1 schema. `internal/manifest`
is the only package that may parse or write `.agent-sync.yaml`.

## File name and location

- Name: **`.agent-sync.yaml`** (literal, lowercase, singular).
- Location: workspace root (the directory the user chose when running
  `agent-sync init`). Discovery semantics are in `internal/workspace`
  (unit 3).

## Schema

```yaml
# --- Required ----------------------------------------------------------

# Manifest schema version. v1 is the only accepted value today.
version: 1

# Canonical agent-config source. Exactly one of `url`, `local_path`, or
# `local_dir` must be set; setting more than one is a load error.
#
#   url        — a remote git repository (cloned + pinned by `commit`).
#   local_path — a local git repository / clone (opened + pinned by `commit`).
#   local_dir  — an in-repo working-tree directory read straight from the
#                filesystem. Unpinned by nature: no `commit`/`ref`/`trusted_sha`,
#                and exempt from trust (TOFU) and offline-strict.
canonical:
  url: https://github.com/example/agents-config.git  # exclusive with local_path/local_dir
  # local_path: /absolute/path/to/a/working/clone     # exclusive with url/local_dir
  # local_dir: .agents                                 # exclusive with url/local_path

  # Optional git ref (branch or tag). `agent-sync init` resolves this to a
  # SHA unless `--defer-resolve` is used.
  ref: main

  # 40-char lowercase hex commit SHA. Pinning is the default — `init`
  # writes this back after resolving `ref`. Mutually required with
  # `trusted_sha` when non-empty.
  commit: 1111111111111111111111111111111111111111

# --- Recommended (required by `--non-interactive` / CI when pinned) ---

# Project-level trust anchor. Committed to git. CI fails closed if the
# resolved SHA drifts from this value. Must match `canonical.commit`
# exactly. See plan decision #9.
trusted_sha: 1111111111111111111111111111111111111111

# --- Optional ---------------------------------------------------------

# floating: reserved; not yet in the v1 schema. Will be added by Unit 5/6.

# Target adapters active for this workspace. Inactive adapters emit
# nothing.
targets:
  - claude
  - cursor
  - codex

# Scope hint informing per-target emission paths. One of:
#   user | project | global
# Empty (default) means no explicit hint.
scope: project

# Cache overrides. Empty means "use user XDG cache".
cache:
  override: /custom/workspace-local/cache

# Adapter source pins (reserved for Unit 20; v1 bundled adapters ignore
# this section). `pin` and `trusted_sha` on adapters are reserved; not
# yet in the v1 schema. Will be added by Unit 20.
adapters:
  - name: claude
    source: github.com/example/agent-sync-adapter-claude

# trust: reserved; not yet in the v1 schema. Fields `require_attestation`
# and `allow_new_shas_until` will be added by Unit 5/6.
trust:

# --- Forward-compat extension keys ------------------------------------

# Keys whose names start with `x-` are explicitly accepted by the
# loader and ignored by core. This provides a stable extension surface
# for community tooling.
x-local-note: any-string-you-like
```

## Validation rules

The loader enforces these at parse time. Any violation returns
`ErrInvalidManifest` wrapping a specific cause.

| Rule | Error text excerpt |
|------|---------------------|
| Unknown top-level key | `unknown field` (goccy formatter names line + field) |
| Not exactly one of `canonical.url` / `local_path` / `local_dir` set | `canonical source must set exactly one of url, local_path, or local_dir (got N)` |
| `canonical.local_dir` set with `commit`/`ref`/`trusted_sha` | `canonical.local_dir is an unpinned working-tree source and cannot set commit, ref, or trusted_sha` |
| `canonical.local_dir` is the workspace root (`.`) | `canonical.local_dir must name a workspace subdirectory, not the workspace root` |
| `canonical.local_dir` is absolute / rooted / contains `..` | `canonical.local_dir: …` (wraps the reserved-prefix path rules) |
| `trusted_sha` set, `canonical.commit` empty | `trusted_sha is set but canonical.commit is empty (no floating-with-pin hybrid)` |
| `canonical.commit` not 40-hex | `canonical.commit must be 40 lowercase hex` |
| `trusted_sha` not 40-hex | `trusted_sha must be 40 lowercase hex` |
| `canonical.commit` ≠ `trusted_sha` | `trusted_sha must mirror canonical.commit` |
| `scope` not in `{"", user, project, global}` | `scope must be one of user|project|global` |
| Non-interactive + `canonical.commit` set but `trusted_sha` empty | `non-interactive mode requires trusted_sha when canonical.commit is set` |
| `version != 1` (and not 0) | `unsupported manifest version N (want 1)` |
| `cache.override` is non-empty and not absolute | `cache.override must be absolute` |
| `cache.override` contains `..` segments | `cache.override must not contain .. segments` |

Rules that are **deliberately** not enforced at load:

- `floating: true` without the `--floating` CLI flag — caught at sync
  level (a manifest marked floating is always parseable; only sync
  refuses if the flag is missing).
- `canonical.commit` reachability from `canonical.ref` on the remote —
  network operation, handled by unit 5.
- Pre-existing keys for `WriteResolvedSHA` — checked only when that
  function is called (returns `ErrKeyMissing`).

## Field reference

### `version` (int, required)

Manifest schema version. v1 is the only accepted value. The loader
defaults `version: 0` → `1` silently during the greenfield phase; once
agent-sync has shipped, this default will be removed and the field becomes
strictly required.

### `canonical` (mapping, required)

Describes where the portable agent-config source lives.

- `canonical.url` (string; exactly one of url/local_path/local_dir): Git URL.
- `canonical.local_path` (string; exactly one of url/local_path/local_dir):
  absolute filesystem path to a git clone. Intended for air-gapped
  environments and agent-sync development itself. Pinned by `commit`.
- `canonical.local_dir` (string; exactly one of url/local_path/local_dir):
  a workspace-relative directory (e.g. `.agents`) whose contents are compiled
  straight from the **working tree** — no git, no clone, no commit. Unpinned by
  nature and exempt from trust (TOFU) and offline-strict, since there is
  nothing remote to fetch, pin, or trust. Must be a clean, non-root,
  workspace-relative path. The `agent-sync-` skill-id prefix is reserved for
  emitted output in the shared `.agents/skills` tree and is not available for
  authored skill ids (the reader skips `skills/agent-sync-*`).
- `canonical.ref` (string, optional): symbolic git ref; only consumed
  by `agent-sync init` to resolve to `commit`. Not valid with `local_dir`.
- `canonical.commit` (string, optional when `floating: true`): 40-char
  lowercase hex SHA. Pinning is the default. Not valid with `local_dir`.

### `trusted_sha` (string, optional)

The per-project trust anchor. Lockfile-style: committed to the repo
alongside `canonical.commit`. CI's `agent-sync trust verify` fails closed if
this drifts from the resolved SHA. **Must** mirror `canonical.commit`
exactly when both are set.

### `floating` (reserved — not yet in v1 schema)

Will be added by Unit 5/6. Explicit opt-in to floating-ref mode. Must be
paired with a CLI `--floating` flag at init/sync (enforced at sync, not load).

### `targets` (seq of string, optional)

Names of active adapters. Default (empty) means "no adapters active" —
a valid state for a workspace that is not yet configured. Adapters not
listed here emit nothing.

### `scope` (string, optional)

One of `user | project | global`. Informs the adapters where the
emitted files live relative to the tool's own scope discovery. Empty
means "no explicit hint."

### `cache` (mapping, optional)

- `cache.override` (string, optional): absolute path overriding the
  default user-cache location. Use when you want the clone to live
  inside the workspace (air-gap, portability).

### `adapters` (seq of mapping, optional)

Out-of-tree adapter source pins. Reserved for Unit 20; v1 bundled
adapters ignore this section.

### `trust` (mapping, optional)

The `trust` mapping is present in the schema as a placeholder but contains
no active fields in v1. Fields `require_attestation` and `allow_new_shas_until`
are reserved and will be added by Unit 5/6.

## Extension keys (`x-*`)

Any top-level key whose name starts with `x-` is allowed by the
loader's `AllowFieldPrefixes("x-")` configuration. agent-sync core ignores
them. Third-party tooling (editor plugins, CI lint rules, workspace
dashboards) can use the prefix to attach additional metadata without
needing a core schema bump.

## Writing the manifest — `WriteResolvedSHA`

`WriteResolvedSHA(orig []byte, commit, trustedSHA string) → []byte`
is the only supported way for agent-sync to mutate an existing manifest.

**Contract:**

1. Input is the exact source bytes of the current manifest.
2. Both target keys (`canonical.commit` and `trusted_sha`) **must already
   exist** in `orig`, with any value (including the empty string). If
   either key is missing, the function returns `ErrKeyMissing` — it
   **never** appends silently into a nested mapping (doing so would
   lose user intent around indentation and ordering).
3. The function updates the scalar values via goccy's AST
   `PathString().ReplaceWithReader`; every comment and key outside the
   targeted nodes is preserved byte-for-byte.
4. A trailing newline is normalized into the output.
5. Pass `""` for either argument to skip updating that key.

`WriteTrustedSHA(orig, trustedSHA)` is a thin alias used by
`agent-sync trust pin`; it updates only `trusted_sha:`.

`WriteFile(path, content)` writes the manifest through
`internal/fsroot.StagedWrite` — atomic, containment-checked, crash-safe.

## Init template

`agent-sync init` emits a template that pre-declares the keys
`WriteResolvedSHA` expects. A minimal example lives at
`testdata/manifest/valid-template-unresolved.yaml`.

## Task 3: Use Manifest Scope In Prepared Requests

**Files:**

- Modify: `internal/cli/setup.go`
- Modify: `internal/cli/hierarchy_sync.go`
- Modify: `internal/cli/hierarchy_sync_test.go`
- Modify: `internal/cli/cmd_validate.go`

- [ ] **Step 1: Write failing request-scope test**

Add to `internal/cli/hierarchy_sync_test.go`:

```go
func TestPrepareScope_UsesManifestScopeWhenPresent(t *testing.T) {
	home := t.TempDir()
	ws := t.TempDir()
	writeWS(t, ws, ".agent-sync.yaml", "version: 1\nscope: workspace\nactivation_root: true\ncanonical:\n  local_dir: .agents\ntargets:\n  - codex\n")
	writeWS(t, ws, ".agents/AGENTS.md", "workspace instructions\n")

	rc := newTestRuntime(t, home)
	now := fixedNow()()
	prep, err := prepareScope(context.Background(), rc, ws, filepath.Join(ws, ".agent-sync.yaml"), "project", now)
	if err != nil {
		t.Fatalf("prepareScope: %v", err)
	}
	defer prep.Close()
	if prep.Request.Scope != "workspace" {
		t.Fatalf("Request.Scope = %q, want workspace", prep.Request.Scope)
	}
}
```

Run:

```bash
go test ./internal/cli -run TestPrepareScope_UsesManifestScopeWhenPresent -count=1
```

Expected: FAIL because `prepareScope` uses the caller-provided scope.

- [ ] **Step 2: Add scope selection helper**

In `internal/cli/setup.go`:

```go
func requestScope(m *manifest.Manifest, discovered string) string {
	if m != nil && m.Scope != "" {
		return m.Scope
	}
	if discovered != "" {
		return discovered
	}
	return manifest.ScopeProject
}
```

Change `engine.Request{Scope: scope}` to:

```go
Scope: requestScope(m, scope),
```

Change `prepareEngine` comment from "always project scope" to "project fallback unless the loaded manifest declares a scope."

- [ ] **Step 3: Ensure Cursor composition remains project-only**

In `prepareEngine`, call:

```go
actualScope := requestScope(prep.Manifest, "project")
applyCursorComposition(ctx, rc, &prep.Request, prep.Manifest, actualScope, home, now)
```

In `runHierarchySync`, call `applyCursorComposition` with `req.Scope` instead of `sc.Level.String()`.

- [ ] **Step 4: Verify CLI scope tests**

Run:

```bash
go test ./internal/cli -run 'TestPrepareScope_UsesManifestScopeWhenPresent|TestCompose' -count=1
```

Expected: PASS.

- [ ] **Step 5: Commit**

```bash
git add internal/cli
git commit -m "fix: honor manifest scope in engine requests"
```

 # Empty (default) means no explicit hint.
 scope: project
 
+# Marks this workspace manifest as an activation boundary for hierarchy
+# discovery. Valid only with `scope: workspace`.
+activation_root: false
+
 # Cache overrides. Empty means "use user XDG cache".
 cache:
   override: /custom/workspace-local/cache
@@
-| `scope` not in `{"", user, workspace, project, global}` | `scope must be one of user|workspace|project|global` |
+| `scope` not in `{"", user, project, workspace, global}` | `scope must be one of user|project|workspace|global` |

 | Non-interactive + `canonical.commit` set but `trusted_sha` empty | `non-interactive mode requires trusted_sha when canonical.commit is set` |
@@
 means "no explicit hint." `global` is accepted as a legacy alias for
 project-level emission.
 
+### `activation_root` (bool, optional)
+
+Marks this manifest as the activation boundary for hierarchy discovery.
+When a sync starts inside an activation root, discovery stops at that
+workspace and does not continue to the user-home root. Valid only with
+`scope: workspace`.
+
 ### `cache` (mapping, optional)
*** End Patch

   226	// It mutates req in place and returns whether rules were composed. All paths are
   227	// gated on m.Compose.CursorRulesFromUser:
   228	//   - scope != project: composition is project-only (D1) — warn that the opt-in
   229	//     has no effect at this scope, return false.
   230	//   - cursor not a target, or no user manifest at home: silent no-op, false.
   231	//   - user source unreadable: defer cursor (drop it from req.Targets) so a
   232	//     transient failure can't wipe previously-composed rules; warn; false.
   233	//   - rules composed: append to req.Nodes; true.
   234	func applyCursorComposition(ctx context.Context, rc *runtimeContext, req *engine.Request, m *manifest.Manifest, scope, home string, now time.Time) bool {
   235		if !m.Compose.CursorRulesFromUser {
   236			return false
   237		}
   238		if scope != hierarchy.LevelProject.String() {
   239			// Directory/user manifest set the opt-in; composition only applies at
   240			// project scope. Warn so it isn't a silent no-op.
   241			rc.Logger.Warn("compose: cursor-rules-from-user has no effect at this scope; set it on the project manifest", "scope", scope)
   242			return false
   243		}
   244		if !slices.Contains(req.Targets, cursorTarget) {
   245			return false
   246		}
   247		user, ok := hierarchy.UserScope(home)
   248		if !ok {
   249			return false // no user manifest to compose from
   250		}
   251		composed, failed := composeUserRules(ctx, rc, user, cursorRuleIDsOf(req.Nodes), now)
   252		switch {
   253		case failed:
   254			// The user source could not be read this run (offline URL, malformed user
   255			// manifest). Defer the Cursor sync rather than syncing project-only rules:
   256			// .cursor/rules/agent-sync/ is an owned subdir replaced by a wholesale
   257			// swap, so a project-only sync would wipe the previously-composed rules.
   258			// Dropping cursor from this run's targets leaves that subdir untouched; the
   259			// next sync that can read the user source re-syncs cursor fully. Project
   260			// cursor rule edits wait for that run — the conservative, data-preserving
   261			// choice (plan D8, transient-failure guard).
   262			req.Targets = withoutTarget(req.Targets, cursorTarget)
   263			rc.Logger.Warn("compose: deferring cursor this run — user source unreadable; existing .cursor/rules left intact",
   264				"user_manifest", user.ManifestPath)
   265			return false
   266		case len(composed) > 0:
   267			req.Nodes = append(req.Nodes, composed...)
   268			return true
   269		}
   270		return false
   271	}
   272	
   273	// composeUserRules materializes the user-scope manifest read-only and returns
   274	// its Cursor `rule` nodes for injection into a project sync (plan D1). The
   275	// project root remains the only write target; the user root is opened solely to
   276	// read IR.
   277	//
   278	// It is best-effort (plan D8): any failure — load, open, materialize, offline
   279	// or uncached remote source, decode — yields nil plus a warning and never
   280	// propagates. A project sync must not fail, block on a trust prompt, or hit the
   281	// network because of the user's global manifest. Remote (URL) user sources are
   282	// materialized OFFLINE (cache-only) so composition never fetches; a user who
   283	// wants a remote source composed pre-populates the cache with `sync --user`.
   284	//
   285	// projectRuleIDs is the set of `rule` ids already present in the project scope.

payload.message: Cursor treats every non-user scope as project paths, so `global` can safely remain a legacy project alias at this layer. I’m adding the regression in `compose_test.go` beside the workspace negative test.

content.text: Cursor treats every non-user scope as project paths, so `global` can safely remain a legacy project alias at this layer. I’m adding the regression in `compose_test.go` beside the workspace negative test.

payload.input:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: internal/cli/compose_test.go
@@
 func TestCompose_WorkspaceManifestDoesNotComposeUserRules(t *testing.T) {
 	home, repo := composeTree(t)
 	if err := os.WriteFile(filepath.Join(home, ".agent-sync.yaml"), []byte(composeUserManifestCursor), 0o644); err != nil {
@@
 	mustNotExist(t, rulePath(repo, "from-user"))
 }
 
+func TestCompose_GlobalManifestComposesAsProjectAlias(t *testing.T) {
+	home, repo := composeTree(t)
+	if err := os.WriteFile(filepath.Join(home, ".agent-sync.yaml"), []byte(composeUserManifestCursor), 0o644); err != nil {
+		t.Fatal(err)
+	}
+	writeWS(t, home, ".agents/rules/from-user.md", "global alias should inherit user rules\n")
+	if err := os.WriteFile(filepath.Join(repo, ".agent-sync.yaml"), []byte(composeProjectGlobalManifest), 0o644); err != nil {
+		t.Fatal(err)
+	}
+	writeWS(t, repo, ".agents/rules/project-only.md", "project rule\n")
+
+	if _, errOut, err := runSyncHierarchy(t, repo, home); err != nil {
+		t.Fatalf("sync failed: %v\nstderr: %s", err, errOut)
+	}
+	mustExist(t, rulePath(repo, "project-only"))
+	mustExist(t, rulePath(repo, "from-user"))
+}
+
 // TestCompose_ComposedRuleDoesNotLeakToOtherAdapters is the regression guard for
 // the empty-Targets over-delivery bug: a user rule authored with no frontmatter
 // targets means "all adapters", but composition is Cursor-only (D1). In a
@@
 const composeProjectWorkspaceManifest = "version: 1\n" +
 	"scope: " + manifest.ScopeWorkspace + "\n" +
 	"canonical:\n" +
 	"  local_dir: .agents\n" +
@@
 	"compose:\n" +
 	"  cursor-rules-from-user: true\n"
 
+const composeProjectGlobalManifest = "version: 1\n" +
+	"scope: " + manifest.ScopeGlobal + "\n" +
+	"canonical:\n" +
+	"  local_dir: .agents\n" +
+	"targets:\n" +
+	"  - cursor\n" +
+	"compose:\n" +
+	"  cursor-rules-from-user: true\n"
+
 // composeCursorNoOptIn is the same project manifest without the opt-in.
 const composeCursorNoOptIn = "version: 1\n" +
 	"canonical:\n" +
*** End Patch

That violates the follow-up global-alias consistency contract.\n- `internal/cli/setup.go:75` still says the single-scope path is “always project scope.” Task 3 Step 2 explicitly required changing this to project fallback unless the loaded manifest declares a scope, and the stale comment now contradicts `requestScope`."}}

Task 3 from the approved implementation plan:

## Task 3: Use Manifest Scope In Prepared Requests

Files planned:
- Modify: internal/cli/setup.go
- Modify: internal/cli/hierarchy_sync.go
- Modify: internal/cli/hierarchy_sync_test.go
- Modify: internal/cli/cmd_validate.go

Step 1: Write failing request-scope test in internal/cli/hierarchy_sync_test.go verifying prepareScope loads a workspace manifest with scope: workspace and activation_root: true, then sets prep.Request.Scope to workspace even if discovered scope is project.

Step 2: Add requestScope helper in internal/cli/setup.go:
func requestScope(m *manifest.Manifest, discovered string) string {
    if m != nil && m.Scope != "" { return m.Scope }
    if discovered != "" { return discovered }
    return manifest.ScopeProject
}
Change engine.Request{Scope: scope} to use requestScope(m, scope). Change prepareEngine comment from always project scope to project fallback unless the loaded manifest declares a scope.

Step 3: Ensure Cursor composition remains project-only:
- In prepareEngine, compute actualScope := requestScope(prep.Manifest, "project") and pass it to applyCursorComposition.
- In runHierarchySync, pass req.Scope to applyCursorComposition instead of sc.Level.String().

Step 4: Verify CLI scope tests pass:
go test ./internal/cli -run 'TestPrepareScope_UsesManifestScopeWhenPresent|TestCompose' -count=1

Step 5: Commit as fix: honor manifest scope in engine requests.

Additional accepted follow-up contract requirements in this task range:
- adapter protocol docs, JSON schema, adapterkit exported types/comments, engine/request comments, and manifest docs should consistently accept user, project, workspace, directory, and legacy global where appropriate.
- manifest scope accepts user, workspace, project, global; global is a legacy/project alias.
- Cursor rule composition remains project-only, but legacy scope: global must behave as the project alias for that composition gate. scope: workspace must not compose user rules.
- activation_root is part of manifest v1 and valid only with scope: workspace.

Please answer with exactly one of:
- APPROVED: if the implementation satisfies Task 3 and the follow-up contract/doc scope consistency requirements.
- CHANGES_REQUIRED: with a concise bullet list of concrete gaps, each with file/line evidence and why it violates the Task 3 spec or public contract.

Be adversarial about contract drift: if any schema, public docs, exported adapterkit types/comments, CLI setup/update/validate/hierarchy paths, or composition tests still encode the old user|project|global-only world or pass discovered hierarchy level instead of manifest scope, call it out."}

{"agent_type":"worker","model":"gpt-5.3-codex-spark","reasoning_effort":"xhigh","fork_context":false,"message":"You are implementing Task 4 in the agent-sync repo at /Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync on branch feat/agent-harness-hierarchy.

You are not alone in the codebase: do not revert edits made by others, and adjust to the current code. Own only these files/modules:
- create internal/harness/types.go
- create internal/harness/decode.go
- create internal/harness/decode_test.go
- modify internal/cli/materialize.go only as needed to include decoded fragments in materialized output

Do not modify other files unless absolutely required; if required, explain why.

Follow TDD: add the failing tests first, confirm they fail because internal/harness does not exist or Decode is missing, then implement. Commit your changes when tests pass.

Task 4: Decode Native Fragments

Plan requirements:
1. Create internal/harness/decode_test.go with tests equivalent to:
- fakeSource implements ir.SourceTree using map[string][]byte and git.TreeEntry.
- TestDecodeFragments_CodexFeatureFlag: source has configs/codex/features/hooks/fragment.yaml:
  id: hooks
  target: codex
  path: .codex/config.toml
  merge: toml-key
  locator: features.hooks
  payload: payload.toml
  and payload.toml contains [features]\nhooks = true\n. Decode(src, "", DecodeOptions{Scope: "workspace"}) returns one fragment, no warnings, identity equals "codex\x00.codex/config.toml\x00toml-key\x00features.hooks", defaults VisibilityTeam/InheritanceDescendants/SafetyPassive.
- TestDecodeFragments_RejectsPayloadTraversal: payload: ../payload.toml returns a non-nil error.
- TestDecodeFragments_UserDefaultsArePersonalRootOnly: scope user defaults VisibilityPersonal and InheritanceRootOnly.

2. Create internal/harness/types.go:
- package harness
- import internal/ir
- define Visibility, Inheritance, Safety, MergeKind string types
- constants:
  VisibilityPersonal = "personal", VisibilityTeam = "team", VisibilityMachineLocal = "machine-local"
  InheritanceRootOnly = "root-only", InheritanceDescendants = "descendants"
  SafetyPassive = "passive", SafetyToolAccess = "tool-access", SafetyExecutable = "executable"
  MergeTOMLKey = "toml-key", MergeCodexHooks = "codex-hooks"
- Fragment struct fields: ID, Target, Path string; Merge MergeKind; Locator string; Visibility Visibility; Inheritance Inheritance; Safety Safety; PayloadPath string; Payload []byte; Scope string; Provenance ir.Provenance.
- Fragment.Identity() returns Target + "\x00" + Path + "\x00" + string(Merge) + "\x00" + Locator.
- Warning struct { Code, Message, Path string }
- DecodeOptions struct { Scope string }

3. Create internal/harness/decode.go:
- Decode(src ir.SourceTree, ref string, opts DecodeOptions) ([]Fragment, []Warning, error)
- Use src.ReadTree(ref) and find files whose path.Base is fragment.yaml and strings.HasPrefix(path, "configs/"). Sort manifest paths deterministically.
- Parse fragment YAML strictly with github.com/goccy/go-yaml and yaml.DisallowUnknownField(). Expected YAML fields: id, target, path, merge, locator, visibility, inheritance, safety, payload.
- buildFragment should validate required fields id,target,path,merge,payload; locator required for merge toml-key and codex-hooks; accepted merge kinds are MergeTOMLKey and MergeCodexHooks.
- payload path is resolved relative to the fragment.yaml directory. Reject empty, absolute/rooted, any path traversal, or paths outside the fragment directory. Use path.Clean/path.Join style for canonical slash paths.
- Read payload bytes with src.BlobContent(ref, payloadPath). Store PayloadPath and Payload.
- Defaults: scope "user" => VisibilityPersonal + InheritanceRootOnly unless explicitly set. Other scopes => VisibilityTeam + InheritanceDescendants unless explicitly set. Safety defaults SafetyPassive.
- Validate visibility/inheritance/safety values against constants when present/defaulted.
- Provenance should identify the fragment manifest path using ir.Provenance{Path: manifestPath}. Scope should be opts.Scope.
- Return warnings nil for now; invalid manifests are errors with useful context including manifest path.

4. Modify internal/cli/materialize.go:
- import internal/harness.
- materialized struct gets Fragments []harness.Fragment.
- decodeAt should call harness.Decode(src, ref, harness.DecodeOptions{Scope: ???}). Since Task 3 now has manifest scope available only at materialize caller, change decodeAt signature/options as needed to pass scope through from materialize(...) callers. For local_dir/local_path/url, pass the loaded manifest's scope if present, else project fallback. Keep SourceURL/Commit/Warn behavior intact.
- Do not wire fragments into engine.Request yet; Task 5 will do that.

- create internal/harness/types.go

Spec requirements:
1. Tests equivalent to these exist and pass:
- Decode a configs/codex/features/hooks/fragment.yaml with id hooks, target codex, path .codex/config.toml, merge toml-key, locator features.hooks, payload payload.toml. payload is [features]\nhooks = true\n. Decode(scope workspace) returns one fragment, no warnings, identity "codex\x00.codex/config.toml\x00toml-key\x00features.hooks", defaults team/descendants/passive.
- Reject payload traversal with payload ../payload.toml.
- Decode(scope user) defaults personal/root-only.

2. types.go exposes:
- Visibility, Inheritance, Safety, MergeKind string types.
- constants: personal/team/machine-local, root-only/descendants, passive/tool-access/executable, toml-key/codex-hooks.
- Fragment fields: ID, Target, Path string; Merge MergeKind; Locator string; Visibility Visibility; Inheritance Inheritance; Safety Safety; PayloadPath string; Payload []byte; Scope string; Provenance ir.Provenance.
- Fragment.Identity() returns Target + NUL + Path + NUL + merge + NUL + Locator.
- Warning { Code, Message, Path string }.
- DecodeOptions { Scope string }.

3. decode.go:
- Decode(src ir.SourceTree, ref string, opts DecodeOptions) ([]Fragment, []Warning, error).
- Uses ReadTree(ref), finds path.Base == fragment.yaml and prefix configs/, sorts deterministically.
- Strict YAML parse with goccy/go-yaml and DisallowUnknownField. Fields id,target,path,merge,locator,visibility,inheritance,safety,payload.
- validates required id,target,path,merge,payload; locator required for toml-key and codex-hooks; accepted merge kinds toml-key and codex-hooks only.
- payload resolved relative to fragment.yaml directory. Reject empty, absolute/rooted, any traversal, or outside fragment directory. Use slash paths.
- Reads payload with BlobContent(ref,payloadPath), stores PayloadPath/Payload.
- Defaults exactly: scope user => personal/root-only; scope workspace => team/descendants; all other scopes including project/global/directory/empty => team/root-only; safety defaults passive.
- Validates visibility/inheritance/safety values.
- Provenance is ir.Provenance{Path: manifestPath}; Scope is opts.Scope.
- Warnings nil for now; invalid manifests are errors with useful context including manifest path.

4. materialize.go:
- imports internal/harness.
- materialized has Fragments []harness.Fragment.
- decodeAt calls harness.Decode and appends any harness warnings to materialized Warnings as ir.Warning.
- materialize callers pass loaded manifest scope if present, else manifest.ScopeProject.
- Does not wire fragments into engine.Request yet.
- Existing SourceURL/Commit/Warn behavior intact.

Local tests already passed:
- go test ./internal/harness -run TestDecodeFragments -count=1
- go test ./internal/cli ./internal/harness -count=1

Please answer exactly:
- APPROVED if the commit satisfies Task 4.
- CHANGES_REQUIRED with concise bullets containing file/line evidence and concrete fix if not.

Be adversarial about hidden spec drift, especially payload path containment, defaulting, strict YAML, deterministic ordering, and materialize scope propagation."}

Task 4: Decode Native Fragments

Spec requirements:
1. Tests equivalent to these exist and pass:
- Decode configs/codex/features/hooks/fragment.yaml with id hooks, target codex, path .codex/config.toml, merge toml-key, locator features.hooks, payload payload.toml. payload is [features]\nhooks = true\n. Decode(scope workspace) returns one fragment, no warnings, identity "codex\x00.codex/config.toml\x00toml-key\x00features.hooks", defaults team/descendants/passive.
- Reject payload traversal with payload ../payload.toml and raw hidden traversal like nested/../payload.toml.
- Decode(scope user) defaults personal/root-only.

2. types.go exposes Visibility/Inheritance/Safety/MergeKind constants, Fragment fields including Payload/Scope/Provenance, Identity, Warning, DecodeOptions as specified.

3. decode.go:
- Decode(src ir.SourceTree, ref string, opts DecodeOptions) ([]Fragment, []Warning, error).
- Uses ReadTree(ref), finds path.Base == fragment.yaml and prefix configs/, sorts deterministically.
- Requires fragment manifests and payloads to be regular file entries from ReadTree before BlobContent, so local_dir skipped symlinks cannot be opened by path.
- Strict YAML parse with goccy/go-yaml and DisallowUnknownField. Fields id,target,path,merge,locator,visibility,inheritance,safety,payload.
- validates required id,target,path,merge,payload; locator required for toml-key and codex-hooks; accepted merge kinds toml-key and codex-hooks only.
- payload resolved relative to fragment.yaml directory. Reject empty, absolute/rooted, any traversal, or outside fragment directory. Use slash paths.
- Reads payload with BlobContent(ref,payloadPath), stores PayloadPath/Payload.
- Defaults exactly: scope user => personal/root-only; scope workspace => team/descendants; all other scopes including project/global/directory/empty => team/root-only; safety defaults passive.
- Validates visibility/inheritance/safety values.
- Does not silently coerce explicit machine-local inheritance.
- Provenance is ir.Provenance{Path: manifestPath}; Scope is opts.Scope.
- Warnings nil for now; invalid manifests are errors with useful context including manifest path.

4. materialize.go:
- imports internal/harness.
- materialized has Fragments []harness.Fragment.
- decodeAt calls harness.Decode and appends any harness warnings to materialized Warnings as ir.Warning.
- materialize callers pass loaded manifest scope if present, else manifest.ScopeProject.
- Does not wire fragments into engine.Request yet.
- Existing SourceURL/Commit/Warn behavior intact.

- go test ./internal/harness -run TestDecodeFragments -count=1
- go test ./internal/cli ./internal/harness -count=1

Task 5: Resolve Inherited Portable Assets And Native Fragments

Core invariants:
- Hierarchy discovery may read user/workspace/project layers, but a sync writes exactly one selected scope.
- Plain sync writes the closest non-user scope. `sync --user` writes only the user scope. Explicit `--workspace` stays single-scope and predictable.
- Activation roots already stop discovery before user, so do not reintroduce user root reads under activation roots.
- User portable nodes/fragments do not inherit into projects by default. Existing compose.cursor-rules-from-user remains the explicit user-to-project exception.
- This task only resolves/inserts fragments into requests; engine does not apply them yet.

Required resolver tests in internal/harness/resolve_test.go:
1. TestResolveNodes_WorkspaceSkillInheritedIntoProject:
   workspace skill node and skill asset inherit into project; inherited node SourceURL becomes layer SourceURL; skill asset present.
2. REDACTED:
   project node shadows workspace node with same kind/id; output contains project body.
3. TestResolveNodes_UserLayerDoesNotInheritIntoProjectByDefault:
   user skill alone resolves to no project nodes.
4. TestResolveFragments_ClosestScopeWinsByIdentity:
   workspace fragment and project fragment same Identity; project payload wins.
5. TestResolveFragments_SkipsPersonalAncestorForProject:
   user personal descendant fragment does not inherit into project.
6. TestResolveFragments_IncludesTeamDescendantAncestor:
   workspace team descendant fragment inherits into project.

Resolver implementation in internal/harness/resolve.go:
- type Layer struct { Scope string; Nodes []ir.Node; Skills map[string]ir.Skill; Fragments []Fragment; SourceURL string; Commit string }
- ResolveNodes(layers []Layer, targetScope string) ([]ir.Node, map[string]ir.Skill)
  - process layers in order from ancestor to current.
  - current layer is len(layers)-1 and always included.
  - non-current layer included only if portableLayerInherits(layer.Scope,targetScope) is true.
  - portableLayerInherits returns true for same scope and for scope == "workspace". Do not inherit user into project.
  - shadow by kind + NUL + id; closer layer overwrites value, but keep first-seen order.
  - for inherited non-current nodes, set SourceURL and SourceCommit from layer.
  - for skill nodes, map matching layer.Skills[n.ID], deleting stale shadowed assets first.
- ResolveFragments(layers []Layer, targetScope string) []Fragment
  - process layers in order; current layer always included.
  - non-current fragments included only if canInherit(f,targetScope): same scope, or f.Inheritance == descendants and f.Visibility == team.
  - shadow by Fragment.Identity(); closer wins, first-seen order retained.

Engine request:
- import internal/harness in internal/engine/request.go.
- Add Request.Fragments []harness.Fragment with comment that these are resolved target-native harness fragments applied by core allowlisted surfaces, not adapters.

Single-scope setup:
- internal/cli/setup.go should set Fragments: mat.Fragments in engine.Request.
- For explicit --workspace, keep only that scope's own materialized fragments/nodes.

Hierarchy sync changes:
- Add selectWriteScopes(scopes []hierarchy.Scope, includeUser bool) []hierarchy.Scope exactly in spirit:
  - copy scopes and set all Emit=false.
  - if includeUser, mark first user scope Emit=true and return.
  - otherwise mark closest non-user scope (iterate from end, skip user) Emit=true and return.
- Call selectWriteScopes immediately after hierarchy.Discover in runHierarchySync.
- Remove the interactive OfferUser path from hierarchySyncOptions/newSyncCommand/runHierarchySync. Plain project sync may read inherited layers but must not offer or perform a second write to $HOME. Notice about skipped user can remain if useful, but no prompt.
- Before the emit loop, materialize all discovered scopes read-only into preparedLayer values:
  type preparedLayer struct { Scope hierarchy.Scope; Materialized materialized; Manifest *manifest.Manifest }
  helper materializeLayerReadOnly(ctx, rc, sc, now) (preparedLayer,bool): LoadFile noninteractive, OpenWorkspaceRoot, materialize with Offline rc.Flags.Offline || URL != "", close root, warn and return false on read/open/materialize failure.
- Build layers in discovery order up to the selected emitted scope:
  layersForScope(sc, preparedLayers) []harness.Layer with Scope p.Scope.Level.String(), Nodes/Skills/Fragments/SourceURL/Commit from materialized, break when ManifestPath matches sc.ManifestPath.
- Add applyResolvedLayers(req *engine.Request, sc hierarchy.Scope, preparedLayers []preparedLayer) that sets req.Nodes, req.Skills = harness.ResolveNodes(...), req.Fragments = harness.ResolveFragments(...).
- In runHierarchySync for emitted scopes, after req := prep.Request and options assignment, call applyResolvedLayers before applyCursorComposition/coverage/engine.Sync.
- In prepareEngine: when rc.Flags.Workspace == "" (validate/default nearest path), use hierarchy discovery plus selectWriteScopes to find selected write scope, prepareScope for that scope, materialize read-only layers, applyResolvedLayers, then apply Cursor composition. - TestSelectWriteScopes_UserFlagSelectsOnlyUser: user/project + includeUser -> only user emits.

- go test ./internal/engine ./internal/cli ./internal/harness -count=1

Task 5: Resolve Inherited Portable Assets And Native Fragments

Required files:
- create internal/harness/resolve.go
- create internal/harness/resolve_test.go
- modify internal/cli/hierarchy_sync.go
- modify internal/cli/cmd_sync.go
- modify internal/cli/setup.go
- modify internal/engine/request.go

Spec requirements:
1. Resolver tests and behavior:
- ResolveNodes: workspace skill inherited into project, inherited node SourceURL/SourceCommit from layer, skill asset present.
- ResolveNodes: project shadows workspace by kind+ID.
- ResolveNodes: user layer does not inherit into project by default.
- ResolveFragments: closest scope wins by Fragment.Identity.
- ResolveFragments: personal ancestor skipped for project.
- ResolveFragments: team+descendants ancestor included.
- Layer type has Scope, Nodes, Skills, Fragments, SourceURL, Commit.
- ResolveNodes processes layers broad-to-close; current layer is always included; non-current included only if portableLayerInherits returns true for same scope or workspace. Shadow by kind+NUL+id, first-seen order retained. Inherited non-current nodes get SourceURL/SourceCommit. Skill map follows shadowing.
- ResolveFragments processes broad-to-close; current layer always included; non-current included only by canInherit (same scope OR descendants+team); shadow by identity, first-seen order retained.

2. Engine request:
- internal/engine/request.go imports internal/harness and adds Request.Fragments []harness.Fragment with comment that these are resolved native harness fragments applied by core allowlisted surfaces, not adapters.

3. Single-scope setup:
- prepareScope sets Fragments: mat.Fragments in engine.Request.
- Explicit --workspace path remains single-scope and predictable: it should use only that root's own materialized nodes/fragments plus existing Cursor composition behavior, not read hierarchy ancestors.

4. Hierarchy sync:
- selectWriteScopes copies scopes, clears Emit, then selects exactly one write target: includeUser selects first user scope only; otherwise closest non-user scope only.
- runHierarchySync calls selectWriteScopes immediately after discovery.
- The interactive user-scope offer/prompt is removed from hierarchySyncOptions/newSyncCommand/runHierarchySync. Plain project sync may read inherited layers but must not become a second write to $HOME. `sync --user` selects user as the single write target.
- Read-only materialization helper loads each discovered scope non-interactively, opens fsroot, materializes with Offline rc.Flags.Offline || URL != "", warns and skips on failures.
- Layers for an emitted scope are built in discovered order up to that scope. The selected/current layer must come from the already-prepared request, not from a potentially skipped/offline read-only materialization. It must use req.Scope as targetScope, not just sc.Level.String(), so manifest scope overrides/legacy global alias are honored.
- In emitted sync, after `req := prep.Request` and options assignment, apply resolved nodes/skills/fragments before Cursor composition, coverage analysis, and engine.Sync.
- Activation roots already removed user from discovery, so do not re-add user root reads.
- Existing compose.cursor-rules-from-user remains explicit user-to-project exception; this task should not replace or broaden it.

5. prepareEngine / validate:
- If rc.Flags.Workspace == "", prepareEngine uses hierarchy discovery + selectWriteScopes to pick the selected write scope, prepareScope for it, materialize read-only layers, apply resolved layers, then Cursor composition. This makes validate match plain sync.
- If rc.Flags.Workspace != "", keep explicit single-scope behavior and use only that scope's own materialized nodes/fragments.

6. Verification commands passed locally:
- go test ./internal/harness ./internal/cli -run 'TestResolve(Nodes|Fragments)|TestSelectWriteScopes|TestApplyResolvedLayers|TestRunHierarchySync|TestCompose' -count=1
- go test ./internal/engine ./internal/cli ./internal/harness -count=1

payload.message: I’m going to make composition accept an optional discovered user scope instead of rediscovering `~`. That lets activation roots and `--user` runs have a hard boundary, while explicit `--workspace` can stay deliberately single-scope.

content.text: I’m going to make composition accept an optional discovered user scope instead of rediscovering `~`. That lets activation roots and `--user` runs have a hard boundary, while explicit `--workspace` can stay deliberately single-scope.

payload.message: I’m also removing the `sync --user` composition probe. With one write target, `--user` should not do project materialization just to suppress a warning; if only the user scope is being written, that warning can remain truthful for that run.

content.text: I’m also removing the `sync --user` composition probe. With one write target, `--user` should not do project materialization just to suppress a warning; if only the user scope is being written, that warning can remain truthful for that run.

Do not edit files. Review only diff range 3c3eef6..HEAD on branch feat/agent-harness-hierarchy. That range is Task 5 plus review fixes.

Task 5: Resolve Inherited Portable Assets And Native Fragments

Spec requirements:
1. Resolver behavior:
- Layer type has Scope, Nodes, Skills, Fragments, SourceURL, Commit.
- ResolveNodes processes layers broad-to-close; current layer is always included; non-current included only if portableLayerInherits returns true for same scope or workspace. Shadow by kind+NUL+id, first-seen order retained. Inherited non-current nodes get SourceURL/SourceCommit. Skill map follows shadowing.
- ResolveFragments processes broad-to-close; current layer always included; non-current included only by canInherit (same scope OR descendants+team); shadow by identity, first-seen order retained.
- Tests cover workspace skill inheritance, project shadowing, user non-inheritance by default, fragment shadowing, personal ancestor skip, and team descendant include.

2. Engine request:
- internal/engine/request.go imports internal/harness and adds Request.Fragments []harness.Fragment with comment that these are resolved native harness fragments applied by core allowlisted surfaces, not adapters.

3. Single-scope setup:
- prepareScope sets Fragments: mat.Fragments in engine.Request.
- Explicit --workspace remains single-scope/predictable: it uses only that root's own materialized nodes/fragments plus existing explicit Cursor composition behavior, not hierarchy ancestors.

4. Hierarchy sync:
- selectWriteScopes copies scopes, clears Emit, then selects exactly one write target: includeUser selects first user scope only; otherwise closest non-user scope only.
- runHierarchySync calls selectWriteScopes immediately after discovery.
- The interactive user-scope offer/prompt is removed from hierarchySyncOptions/newSyncCommand/runHierarchySync.
- Plain project sync may read inherited layers but must not become a second write to $HOME. sync --user selects user as the single write target.
- Read-only materialization helper loads each discovered scope non-interactively, opens fsroot, materializes with Offline rc.Flags.Offline || URL != "", and returns errors to caller.
- Do not call engine.Sync with an incomplete effective non-user ancestor layer set: if a required non-user ancestor layer fails to materialize, selected sync/validate must fail before mutation so inherited outputs are not orphan-deleted. Existing user composition best-effort behavior may remain for unreadable user scope.
- Layers for an emitted scope are built in discovered order up to that scope. The selected/current layer must come from the already-prepared request, not from read-only materialization. It must use req.Scope as targetScope, not just sc.Level.String(), so manifest scope overrides/legacy global alias are honored.
- In emitted sync, after req := prep.Request and options assignment, apply resolved nodes/skills/fragments before Cursor composition, coverage analysis, and engine.Sync.
- Activation roots already removed user from discovery; composition must respect that and not rediscover/read home behind the hierarchy's back.
- Existing compose.cursor-rules-from-user remains explicit user-to-project exception outside activation roots; this task should not replace or broaden it.
- sync --user must not prepare/materialize/discover adapters for project scopes merely to suppress user-scope coverage warnings.

5. prepareEngine / validate:
- If rc.Flags.Workspace == "", prepareEngine uses hierarchy discovery + selectWriteScopes to pick the selected write scope, prepareScope for it, materialize read-only layers, fail on required non-user ancestor materialization errors, apply resolved layers, then Cursor composition gated by discovered user scope. This makes validate match plain sync.
- If rc.Flags.Workspace != "", keep explicit single-scope behavior and use only that scope's own materialized nodes/fragments.

6. Review-fix regression tests should cover:
- activation root stops user composition even when project compose.cursor-rules-from-user is enabled.

docs/plans/2026-07-06-001-feat-init-defaults-target-discovery-plan.md:12:Make `agent-sync init` work with zero flags: when no source flag is given the canonical source defaults to `local_dir: .agents`, and when no `--target` is given the target list is discovered by probing the destination workspace for each bundled adapter's reserved-prefix directory (`.claude`, `.cursor`, `.codex`, `.pi`, `.agent`) and snapshotted into the manifest. Explicit flags override everything; the wizard keeps running for bare `init` on a TTY and gains discovery-informed defaults. Same update: plain `agent-sync sync` on a TTY offers to include the user-level (`~`) manifest instead of silently skipping it, and every run that skips the user scope says so — no more remembering `--user`.
docs/plans/2026-07-06-001-feat-init-defaults-target-discovery-plan.md:52:- R16. Interactive plain `sync` (TTY, no `--non-interactive`, no `--workspace`, no `--post-merge`, no `--user`) that discovers a user-level manifest offers to include it: "Also sync the user-level manifest at ~/.agent-sync.yaml? [Y/n]". Enter/`y` includes the user scope in the same run; `n` declines for this run. Non-interactive, accessible, piped, watch, and git-hook paths never prompt (AGENTS.md invariant #3 unchanged).
docs/plans/2026-07-06-001-feat-init-defaults-target-discovery-plan.md:53:- R17. Any sync that discovers a user-level manifest but does not emit it (non-interactive run, or the prompt was unavailable) ends with a persistent notice — "user-level manifest at ~/.agent-sync.yaml was not synced; pass --user to include it" — in text output and in the existing additive `notice` JSON field, even when project scopes synced successfully (today the hint fires only on zero-emit runs). A user who explicitly declined the R16 prompt is not nagged with the notice in the same run.
docs/plans/2026-07-06-001-feat-init-defaults-target-discovery-plan.md:70:- **The user-scope offer is a per-run prompt, default yes, gated on the centralized `tui.Interactive` check.** Pressing Enter at an explicit "sync the user scope too?" question is explicit consent, so the "plain sync never writes home" invariant becomes "never writes home without explicit consent (`--user` or an answered prompt)". The prompt is injected into the hierarchy orchestrator as a callback (`nil` = never ask), so `runHierarchySync` stays TTY-free and testable — the same seam pattern as `InitConfig`. No persistence of the answer (YAGNI; `--user` remains the scriptable path).
docs/plans/2026-07-06-001-feat-init-defaults-target-discovery-plan.md:175:- **Approach:** `hierarchySyncOptions` gains `OfferUser func(manifestPath string) bool` (nil = never ask). Inside `runHierarchySync`, when discovery returns a user scope with `Emit == false` and the callback is non-nil, invoke it once before the scope loop; on `true`, re-discover with `IncludeUser: true` (cheap — a couple of stats). `cmd_sync` wires the callback only when `tui.Interactive(...)` and none of `--user`/`--workspace`/`--post-merge` are in play; the prompt itself reads a `[Y/n]` line from stdin and writes to stderr (data/stdout stays clean for `--output json` piping, which is non-interactive anyway).
docs/plans/2026-07-06-001-feat-init-defaults-target-discovery-plan.md:182:  - `--user` set → callback never invoked (already included).
docs/plans/2026-07-06-001-feat-init-defaults-target-discovery-plan.md:192:- **Approach:** Generalize the notice computation: after the scope loop, if discovery saw a user scope that was not emitted and the R16 prompt did not decline it this run, set the notice ("user-level manifest at <path> was not synced; pass --user to include it"). Zero-emit runs keep their existing messages. `renderHierarchyText` prints the notice after the scope blocks when outcomes exist (prefix `note:`), keeping the `nothing to sync:` prefix for zero-emit; JSON reuses the additive `notice` field unchanged.
docs/plans/2026-07-06-001-feat-init-defaults-target-discovery-plan.md:196:  - Same but `--user` set → no notice, two labeled scope blocks (pins R18: exactly one `== level: root ==` header per scope, one status-line set per scope).
docs/plans/2026-07-06-001-feat-init-defaults-target-discovery-plan.md:221:- An `init --user` scope flag.
docs/plans/2026-07-02-001-feat-skill-descriptions-upstream-update-plan.md:40:- R8. After a successful re-pin, update runs the normal sync for the affected scope so files land in the same invocation. Scope selection matches sync's conventions (nearest workspace by default, `--user` for the home manifest, `--workspace` override).
docs/plans/2026-07-02-001-feat-skill-descriptions-upstream-update-plan.md:181:**Verification:** a real `sync --user` followed by Claude Code listing shows the authored description, AND a description-less skill still lists and loads (manual checks, both noted in PR); engine co-emission guard green.
docs/plans/2026-07-02-001-feat-skill-descriptions-upstream-update-plan.md:212:**Approach:** Resolve the workspace manifest (same selection semantics as sync: nearest workspace, `--workspace`, `--user`). Acquire the per-workspace run lock *first* — it covers the manifest write AND the sync phase as one critical section; refuse up front if held. For `url` sources: `cache.Canonicalize` → fetch → `ResolveRef` (manifest `ref` when set; else remote HEAD, with the resolved ref name surfaced in the gate and written back to `canonical.ref` on acceptance, per R6) → fast-forward guard `IsAncestor(oldPin, newSHA)` per R10 → compare to pin. Gate: interactive confirm rendering `old → new` SHAs, the resolved ref name, and a change summary between the two commits from the local mirror (shortlog/diff-stat shape, per R7); non-interactive requires an explicit acceptance flag (decide exact name during implementation; mirror the `--accept-new-source` remediation-message pattern from `internal/trust/policy.go`; the non-fast-forward override is a separate, scarier flag). Write via `manifest.WriteResolvedSHA` (atomic, comment-preserving, both keys). Then invoke the same engine path sync uses for that scope, under the already-held lock. A sync failure after the re-pin follows R8b: distinct exit code, "pin moved, files did not land — re-run `agent-sync sync`" message. `--offline` refuses up front. `local_dir` reports "nothing to pin" cleanly. `local_path` resolves the ref locally from the pinned local repository (no network), mirroring init's local `resolvePin` path (`git.ResolveLocalRef`).
docs/plans/2026-07-02-001-feat-skill-descriptions-upstream-update-plan.md:227:- `--user`: updates the home manifest and syncs the user scope.
docs/plans/2026-07-02-001-feat-skill-descriptions-upstream-update-plan.md:242:**Approach:** README command table gains `update`; quickstart shows the author-a-description flow and the update loop (`agent-sync update --user`); CHANGELOG Unreleased entries for both workstreams. Note the compatibility line: canonical repos that author `description:` require agent-sync ≥ this release (older binaries reject unknown frontmatter keys).
docs/plans/2026-06-17-002-feat-multi-scope-sync-plan.md:5:**Goal:** Make `agent-sync sync` emit every manifest in the discovered hierarchy (cwd → project root), each to its own filesystem scope, with a `--user` flag to also emit the user-home scope, and continue-and-report semantics across scopes.
docs/plans/2026-06-17-002-feat-multi-scope-sync-plan.md:11:This is Plan 2 of 3 for the hierarchy-aware-manifests design (`docs/brainstorms/2026-06-17-hierarchy-aware-manifests-design.md`). It implements the **multi-root emit orchestration** and `--user`. The **coverage analyzer / status hierarchy view** is Plan 3.

# Agent Harness Hierarchy Design

## Goal

Make agent-sync the canonical manager for agent harnesses across user,
workspace, and project scopes without becoming a generic dotfiles copier or
inventing a parallel configuration language for every supported tool.

Agent-sync should manage both:

- portable agent assets that can be translated across tools; and
- tool-native runtime configuration fragments that stay native but are merged,
  inherited, validated, ledgered, and explained safely.

## Product Model

Agent-sync has three scope roots:

| Scope | Root | Created by | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| `user` | `~` | `agent-sync init --user` | Personal defaults outside activation roots. |
| `workspace` | arbitrary directory | `agent-sync init --workspace <dir>` | A shared or personal workspace harness boundary. |
| `project` | repo/project directory | normal `init` / cwd discovery | Project-specific team harness. |

A workspace can declare itself as an activation root:

```yaml
version: 1
scope: workspace
activation_root: true
canonical:
  local_dir: .agents
```

An activation root is a hard stop. When cwd is inside that tree, the user root
is not considered.

Examples:

```text
cwd: ~/src/small-repo
active hierarchy: user -> project

cwd: ~/ActualReality
active hierarchy: workspace

cwd: ~/ActualReality/apps/api
active hierarchy: workspace -> project
```

Nested activation roots are invalid. `init` and `validate` should reject them;
discovery should fail closed and name the conflicting manifests if a broken
tree already contains more than one.

This preserves the existing no-cross-workspace-sync invariant. A sync invocation
has one write target. Ancestor scopes are read-only inputs to the current
target's output plan; they are not synced or mutated.

## Write Model

The write target is selected by the command:

| Command | Writes under | Reads as inputs |
|---|---|---|
| `agent-sync sync --user` | `~` | user scope only |
| `agent-sync sync --workspace ~/ActualReality` | `~/ActualReality` | workspace scope only if activation root; otherwise user -> workspace |
| `agent-sync sync` in a repo outside activation roots | project root | user -> project |
| `agent-sync sync` in a repo inside an activation root | project root | workspace -> project |

Ancestor read failures should not unexpectedly change the failure model of the
current target. During descendant sync, ancestor reads are cache/local-only by
default. If an ancestor source cannot be read without network fetch, TOFU prompt,
or other interactive work, sync continues with a warning unless the descendant
explicitly marks the inherited item as required.

## Resolution Rule

The effective harness is built broadest to closest, within the active hierarchy.

For portable assets:

```text
identity = kind + id
```

For managed native fragments:

```text
identity = target + output path + merge locator
```

Closest scope wins for the same identity. Different identities aggregate.

Example:

```text
workspace:
  skill/code-review
  fragment/codex/.codex/hooks.json#/hooks/PreToolUse

project:
  skill/code-review
  rule/no-friday-deploys
```

Effective project harness:

```text
skill/code-review                              from project
rule/no-friday-deploys                        from project
fragment/codex/.codex/hooks.json#/hooks/...   from workspace
```

The project skill replaces the workspace skill because it has the same
`kind + id`. The hook remains because no closer fragment owns the same target
path and locator.

## Authoring Strategy

Agent-sync should not model every option in Codex, Claude, Cursor,
Antigravity, or Pi. Tool-specific runtime config changes too quickly for a
parallel agent-sync schema to remain accurate.

Use two layers instead.

### Portable Assets

Keep the existing IR-backed portable concepts:

```text
- [ ] **Step 4: Pass current-scope fragments in single-scope setup**

In `internal/cli/setup.go`, set:

```go
Fragments: mat.Fragments,
```

This makes `sync --workspace` and `validate --workspace` apply fragments authored in that root before inherited resolution is added.

- [ ] **Step 5: Resolve ancestor layers in hierarchy sync**

In `internal/cli/hierarchy_sync.go`, first make write-target selection explicit. Discovery returns every active scope, but sync writes exactly one scope:

```go
func selectWriteScopes(scopes []hierarchy.Scope, includeUser bool) []hierarchy.Scope {
	out := append([]hierarchy.Scope(nil), scopes...)
	for i := range out {
		out[i].Emit = false
	}
	if includeUser {
		for i := range out {
			if out[i].Level == hierarchy.LevelUser {
				out[i].Emit = true
				return out
			}
		}
		return out
	}
	for i := len(out) - 1; i >= 0; i-- {
		if out[i].Level == hierarchy.LevelUser {
			continue
		}
		out[i].Emit = true
		return out
	}
	return out
}
```

Call it immediately after discovery:

```go
scopes = selectWriteScopes(scopes, opts.IncludeUser)
```

Remove the interactive "also sync the user-level manifest" offer from `newSyncCommand` and `runHierarchySync`. A plain project sync may read inherited layers, but it must not turn into a second write to `$HOME`. `sync --user` selects the user scope as the single write target.

Then, before the emit loop, materialize each discovered scope read-only into a lightweight layer:

```go
type preparedLayer struct {
	Scope hierarchy.Scope
	Materialized materialized
	Manifest *manifest.Manifest
}
```

Add helper:

```go
func materializeLayerReadOnly(ctx context.Context, rc *runtimeContext, sc hierarchy.Scope, now time.Time) (preparedLayer, bool) {
	m, err := manifest.LoadFile(sc.ManifestPath, manifest.LoadOptions{NonInteractive: true})
	if err != nil {
		rc.Logger.Warn("harness: cannot read ancestor manifest", "path", sc.ManifestPath, "err", err)
		return preparedLayer{}, false
	}
	root, err := fsroot.OpenWorkspaceRoot(sc.Root)
	if err != nil {
		rc.Logger.Warn("harness: cannot open ancestor root", "root", sc.Root, "err", err)
		return preparedLayer{}, false
	}
	defer func() { _ = root.Close() }()
	offline := rc.Flags.Offline || m.Canonical.URL != ""
	mat, err := materialize(ctx, m, materializeOptions{Offline: offline, Now: now, Root: root})
	if err != nil {
		rc.Logger.Warn("harness: cannot materialize ancestor", "path", sc.ManifestPath, "err", err)
		return preparedLayer{}, false
	}
	return preparedLayer{Scope: sc, Materialized: mat, Manifest: m}, true
}
```

Build layers in discovery order. For each emitted scope, resolve portable nodes, skill assets, and fragments from all layers up to that scope:

```go

<collaboration_mode># Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

Use the `request_user_input` tool only when it is listed in the available tools for this turn.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.
</collaboration_mode>

<apps_instructions>
## Apps (Connectors)
Apps (Connectors) can be explicitly triggered in user messages in the format `[$app-name](app://{connector_id})`. Apps can also be implicitly triggered as long as the context suggests usage of available apps.
An app is equivalent to a set of MCP tools within the `codex_apps` MCP.
An installed app's MCP tools are either provided to you already, or can be lazy-loaded through the `tool_search` tool. If `tool_search` is available, the apps that are searchable by `tools_search` will be listed by it.
Do not additionally call list_mcp_resources or list_mcp_resource_templates for apps.
</apps_instructions>

  1) After deciding to use a skill, the main agent must expand the listed short `path` with the matching alias from `### Skill roots`, then open and read its `SKILL.md` completely before taking task actions. If a read is truncated or paginated, continue until EOF.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the directory containing that expanded `SKILL.md` first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, use its routing instructions to identify the files required for the task. The main agent must read each required instruction or reference file itself before acting on it. Do not delegate reading, summarizing, or interpreting skill instructions to a subagent. Subagents may still perform task work when the selected skill allows it.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Progressive disclosure applies to selecting relevant files, not partially reading a selected instruction file. Do not load unrelated references, scripts, or assets.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.
</skills_instructions>

## Plugins
A plugin is a local bundle of skills, MCP servers, and apps.
### How to use plugins
- Skill naming: If a plugin contributes skills, those skill entries are prefixed with `plugin_name:` in the Skills list.
- MCP naming: Plugin-provided MCP tools keep standard MCP identifiers such as `mcp__server__tool`; use tool provenance to tell which plugin they come from.
- Trigger rules: If the user explicitly names a plugin, prefer capabilities associated with that plugin for that turn.
- Relationship to capabilities: Plugins are not invoked directly. Use their underlying skills, MCP tools, and app tools to help solve the task.

# AGENTS.md instructions for /Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync

<INSTRUCTIONS>
# AGENTS.md
## Stack

| Area | Stack |
|------|-------|
| Frontend | Bun + Next.js (App Router) + React 19 + TypeScript strict |
| Backend | Go 1.24+ + Echo v4 + oapi-codegen strict + pgx + sqlc + goose |
| Data / Python | Python 3.12+ + uv + FastAPI + Pydantic v2 + Ruff + mypy strict |
| DB | PostgreSQL (Neon or Railway) |

## Commands

```bash
# TypeScript
bun run dev | build | test | lint
bun run db:push | db:studio

# Go
go run ./cmd/server
go test -race -cover ./...
go vet ./... && golangci-lint run
go generate ./...
goose -dir migrations up

# Python
uv sync
uv run pytest --cov=src
uv run ruff check . && uv run mypy .
uv run marimo edit notebooks/eda.py
```

## Verification (run before declaring done)

Every change must pass its stack's gate. Address root causes, not symptoms. If a test fails, fix the code, not the test.

- TS: `bun run lint && bun run test`
- Go: `go vet ./... && go test -race ./... && golangci-lint run`
- Python: `uv run ruff check . && uv run mypy . && uv run pytest`

## Workflow

Follow the sprint sequence: **Think → Plan → Build → Review → Test → Ship → Reflect.** Skip phases only for trivial changes (typos, rename, single-line fix).

**Superpowers is the default for plan creation and execution.** Reach for gstack skills when they provide a specialized lens Superpowers doesn't.

### When to reach for gstack before or alongside Superpowers

- **Fuzzy product idea that needs reframing, not just implementation**: run `/office-hours` first. It challenges the premise and often reshapes the request. Then hand the design doc to Superpowers for execution.
- **Design-heavy work (net-new UI, visual system, mockup exploration)**: run `/design-consultation` for a system from scratch, or `/design-shotgun` to explore variants. Use `/design-html` to turn an approved mockup into shippable code.
- **DX work (API, CLI, SDK, developer docs)**: run `/plan-devex-review` before building, `/devex-review` after.
- **Debugging with unclear root cause**: run `/investigate`. Follow its iron law — no fixes without investigation.
- **Cross-session pattern capture**: use `/learn` to record project-specific conventions and pitfalls as they emerge.

### Core workflow rules

- **Plan before building.** For any change touching 2+ files, new endpoints, or new external integrations: use Superpowers to produce a plan, wait for approval, then execute. If you can describe the diff in one sentence, skip planning.
- **Reference existing patterns.** Before creating a new service/gateway/repo, read the nearest existing example in the same layer and follow its shape. Don't invent a new shape unless the existing one is wrong.
- **TDD for services, domain logic, and handlers.** Write the failing test first. Describe or write the test, confirm it fails, then implement against it.
- **Review before shipping.** Run `/review` on every branch before opening a PR. Use `/codex` for a second opinion on high-stakes changes. Use `/cso` for security-sensitive surfaces. Use `/design-review` for UI changes.
- **Verify with evidence, not assumptions.** Run the verification command. Read the complete output. Confirm results support your claim. Never declare "done" or "tests pass" without execution proof. If the output doesn't exist, the claim doesn't hold.
- **Debug systematically.** Investigate root cause → analyze patterns → test hypotheses one variable at a time → implement. If 3+ fix attempts fail, stop and question the architecture. Never shotgun-debug.
- **Parallelize independent work.** For tasks with 3+ independent sub-problems, dispatch parallel agents with focused instructions and isolated context. Two-stage review per agent: spec compliance first, then code quality.
- **Evaluate review feedback with rigor.** No performative agreement. Push back when suggestions are technically unsound or use APIs that don't exist in the project's pinned versions. YAGNI-check suggested additions — if it's not used in the codebase, question necessity. Cite evidence (version constraints, existing patterns, test coverage).
- **Ship and verify in prod.** Use `/ship` to open the PR, `/land-and-deploy` to merge and verify production health.
- **Reflect weekly.** Run `/retro` to review per-person shipping velocity, test health, and growth opportunities.

## Architecture (non-negotiable)

Every request flows: **Handler/Route → Service → (Repository | Gateway) → I/O**. Never skip layers.

| Layer | Owns | Imports allowed | Imports forbidden |
|-------|------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Handler/Route | HTTP parsing, auth, response shaping | Service interfaces | Repository, Gateway, DB/HTTP clients |
| Service | Business logic, orchestration, transactions | Repo/Gateway interfaces, Domain | Framework types, DB/HTTP clients |
| Repository | Internal persistence (our DB) | DB client, Domain | Business logic, external APIs, other Repositories |
| Gateway | External systems (APIs, SDKs, queues) | HTTP client, vendor SDKs, Domain | Business logic, DB client, other Gateways |
| Domain | Types, value objects, sentinel errors | Standard library only | Everything else |

### Rules that don't bend

1. Start a feature by defining the Service interface the Handler needs. Work outward.
2. Services depend on **interfaces**, never concrete types. Wire concretes at the composition root (`cmd/main.go` in Go, route/page entry in TS, app factory in Python).
3. Services own transaction boundaries. Repositories accept a transaction handle; they never open their own.
4. Gateways normalize vendor responses into Domain types. Callers never see SDK types.
5. Gateway calls that mutate external state must be idempotent (idempotency key or natural dedupe).
6. Handlers map Domain to API DTOs. Services never see API DTOs.
7. Generated code (`*.gen.go`, sqlc output, Drizzle introspection) is never hand-edited.
8. One LLM gateway per project owns all LLM instrumentation. Services never import Langfuse or LangSmith.
9. Errors return as values inside Service and below. Thrown/panicked only at the Handler boundary.
10. Go: every I/O function takes `context.Context` first. Propagate through every Service → Repo/Gateway call.
11. Don't abstract before the third duplication.

### Naming

- `[domain].service.{ts,go,py}` under `services/`
- `[domain].repository.*` under `repositories/`
- `[provider].gateway.*` under `gateways/`, named by the external system (`stripe.gateway.ts`), not the capability

### File structure

- TS: `src/{app,components,services,repositories,gateways,lib,db}/`
- Go: `cmd/server/main.go` (wiring only), `internal/{handler,service,repository,gateway,domain,platform}/`, `api/openapi.yaml`, `migrations/`
- Python: `src/project_name/{services,repositories,gateways,domain,pipelines}/`, `notebooks/`, `tests/`, `data/` (gitignored)

## Conventions that differ from defaults

- **TS**: `satisfies` over `as`. Any `as` cast requires a `// SAFETY:` comment justifying it. `Result<T, E>` for error returns in the Service layer. Server Components by default.
- **Go**: OpenAPI (`api/openapi.yaml`) is the single source of truth. Handlers implement generated `ServerInterface`. Register `OapiRequestValidator` middleware. Interfaces defined at the consumer, not the implementer. `t.Cleanup` over `defer` in tests.
- **Python**: `uv` is mandatory. No `pip install`, no `requirements.txt`, no Poetry, no Conda. Pydantic at I/O boundaries, dataclasses internal. `src/` layout. Notebooks (marimo preferred) explore, `src/` ships. `data/` is gitignored.

## Testing

- 80% line coverage floor, enforced in CI.
- Mock at architectural boundaries only (Repository/Gateway interfaces). Never mock the thing under test or language primitives.
- AAA structure, one Act per test. Test names describe behavior.
- TS: Vitest + `@testing-library/react`. Test user-visible behavior, not internal state.
- Go: table-driven with `t.Run`. `testify/require` for preconditions, `testify/assert` for checks. `testcontainers-go` for real Postgres in integration tests. Always `-race`.
- Python: plain `assert`. `pytest.mark.parametrize`. `pytest-mock` for boundary mocks. `pytest-xdist -n auto` for parallel.
- Flaky tests are bugs. Fix or delete within 24 hours.

## Logging and observability

- Structured JSON (Pino / slog / structlog). One logger per process.
- Every HTTP request gets a `requestId` in middleware. Propagates via child loggers and as `X-Request-Id` on outbound Gateway calls.
- PII redacted in logger config, not at call sites.
- OTel for traces. Auto-instrumentation covers HTTP/DB/fetch. Add manual spans only in the LLM gateway and in business-critical Service methods (Rule of Three applies).
- Every LLM generation records tokens and cost.

## Git

- Atomic commits, one logical change each.
- Conventional commits: `feat:`, `fix:`, `refactor:`, `test:`, `docs:`, `chore:`.
- Squash feature branches on merge.

## gstack skills (self-hosted)

Skills adapted from [gstack](https://github.com/garrytan/gstack), self-hosted in `skills/gstack/`. Use Playwright MCP (`mcp__playwright__*`) for all browser interaction — see `skills/gstack/BROWSER.md` for the command mapping.

These cover the **post-build lifecycle** (review, QA, ship, reflect) and provide **specialized lenses** for planning that Superpowers doesn't. See Workflow for when each applies.

Default routing:
- Code review before shipping: `/review`, then `/codex` for a second opinion on high-stakes changes.
- Addressing review comments on an existing PR: `pr-feedback-resolver` skill. Handles human and AI reviewer comments — evaluates, implements fixes or dismisses with evidence, replies and resolves threads. Use `/review` *before* opening the PR; use `pr-feedback-resolver` *after* reviewers have commented.
- Security-sensitive surface: `/cso`.
- Design review on UI changes: `/design-review` (live) or `/plan-design-review` (pre-build).
- DX review on API/CLI/SDK changes: `/devex-review` (live) or `/plan-devex-review` (pre-build).
- Root-cause debugging: `/investigate`.
- Product reframing on fuzzy ideas: `/office-hours`.
- Design system / mockup pipeline: `/design-consultation`, `/design-shotgun`, `/design-html`.
- Cross-session memory: `/learn`.
- Browser-based QA on staging/prod: `/qa`, `/qa-only` (report-only, no fixes).
- Shipping: `/ship`, then `/land-and-deploy` once approved.
- Post-deploy monitoring: `/canary`.
- Weekly reflection: `/retro`.
- Doc drift after shipping: `/document-release`.

Safety: `/careful` (destructive command interception), `/freeze` + `/unfreeze` (directory-scoped edit restriction), `/guard` (combined safety mode).

## Other skills

- `superpowers` — brainstorming and plan execution. Default for all planning and build work (see Workflow).
- `plane-time-tracking` — timer start/stop (see top of file, mandatory).
- `creative-frontend-design` — UI work requiring visual polish or design system decisions. Compose with `/design-shotgun` and `/design-html` when both apply.
- `pr-feedback-resolver` — resolving human/AI review comments on an existing PR. Multi-agent pipeline: evaluates and deduplicates feedback, implements tactical fixes, dismisses with evidence-based rationale, replies and resolves threads. Escalates strategic disagreements and questions to the user. Use after a PR has been reviewed, not for pre-merge review (that's `/review`).

<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch

- AskUserQuestion/Question: present choices as a numbered list in chat and wait for a reply number. For multi-select (multiSelect: true), accept comma-separated numbers. Never skip or auto-configure — always wait for the user's response before proceeding.
